content:
Hi all,

I'm working on making it possible to write modules for the grub bootloader in Rust (https://lists.gnu.org/archive/html/grub-devel/2021-08/msg00099.html).

There's a whole bunch of work the Rust for Linux project has done that is potentially really useful and relevant to me - things like your ctypes module for bindgen. However, the code is licensed under GPL2 only. Frustratingly Grub is GPLv3+, which is not compatible with GPLv2. Is it possible to dual-license the code so I can reuse it, or has that ship already sailed?

Rust modules for GRUB sounds neat! Thanks for working on that.

On licensing, you mean GRUB forces to use GPLv3+ for all contributed code? If I am reading https://www.gnu.org/licenses/gpl-faq.html#AllCompatibility right, you can re-use the GPLv2 code.

But yeah, that would force to make GRUB GPLv2-or-later. If that is the problem, given how straightforward that file is and that just a few people have touched the file, I don't think it would be a problem giving you permission to simply copy & relicense it.

About relicensing it inside the kernel-tree (so that you can reuse it in the future), I think the easiest would be to relicense it to GPLv2+, rather than dual licensing it (there is no support for GPLv3 etc. in the kernel tree that I can see). That would allow you to relicense it under GPLv3+ inside GRUB, AFAICT from the matrix.

Yes, I think to reuse the kernel code grub2 would have to relax its policies to allow GPLv2 code, and I'm not sure that's very likely.

If code can be relicensed in the kernel that would be the most helpful, but otherwise I can just contact the copyright holders for any significant files directly. It's _probably_ just the ctypes module that I want to copy in its entirety. I think I want to copy your approach of not using cargo, but I don't think I'll be directly copying kernel makefiles to do so.

Yeah, agreed.

I think the easiest is that we give you permission for the moment, and then if your changes are accepted in GRUB and you still want to reuse files as-is, then we can discuss relicensing a set of files that can be useful for other projects.
Actually, I saw that you are will be at LPC giving a talk, so perhaps we could raise this question there.

Thanks, I'll try integrating your work and touch base again when I know precisely which bits I want to copy.

I'm hoping to do the talk, it hasn't been accepted yet :)

Thanks again. I'm off for the night, talk again soon.
I believe GPLv2 and v3 are not compatible. All contributors would have to agree to dual-license their contributions to GPLv3+ if you want to reuse the code.
Yes I think that's right. Hopefully the list of contributors for the bits I want to copy is short and they're agreeable.

Gary Guo said:

I believe GPLv2 and v3 are not compatible. All contributors would have to agree to dual-license their contributions to GPLv3+ if you want to reuse the code.

Given the matrix, I believe agreeing to GPLv2+ would be enough. And, for us, it would also be required, because GPLv3 is not supported.

Ah yes, sorry, GPLv2+ would be fine, GPLv3+ would not be required.

I realised just now that grub is still a bit of a more traditional FSF project, so I'll have to ask how strictly we're doing the traditional and very irritating 'assign copyright to the FSF' thing.
Asking people to relicense code from GPLv2 to GPLv2+ is one thing, I'm not comfortable asking people to assign any copyright.

Miguel Ojeda said:

Given the matrix, I believe agreeing to GPLv2+ would be enough. And, for us, it would also be required, because GPLv3 is not supported.

Well, dual license GPLv2 and GPLv3+ is effectively GPLv2+ :grinning:
FSF copyright assignment is indeed very annoying.

Daniel Axtens said:

Ah yes, sorry, GPLv2+ would be fine, GPLv3+ would not be required.

I realised just now that grub is still a bit of a more traditional FSF project, so I'll have to ask how strictly we're doing the traditional and very irritating 'assign copyright to the FSF' thing.

Both GCC and glibc have recently moved away from that, so you might consider quietly reaching out to the GRUB maintainers to see if they'd consider following suit.
